question_text: A teacher shows a wave diagram of a transverse wave on a string. The distance from one peak to the next peak is 2 cm. A student counts 6 complete waves passing a fixed point every 3 seconds. Which calculation correctly finds the wave speed?

options.label: A
options.text: Frequency = 6 waves ÷ 3 seconds = 2 Hz; wavelength = 2 cm; speed = 2 × 2 = 4 cm/s

options.label: B
options.text: Frequency = 3 seconds ÷ 6 waves = 0.5 Hz; wavelength = 4 cm; speed = 0.5 × 4 = 2 cm/s

options.label: C
options.text: Frequency = 6 waves; wavelength = 2 cm; speed = 6 × 2 = 12 cm/s

options.label: D
options.text: Frequency = 2 Hz; wavelength = 3 cm; speed = 2 × 3 = 6 cm/s

correct_answer: A) Frequency = 6 waves ÷ 3 seconds = 2 Hz; wavelength = 2 cm; speed = 2 × 2 = 4 cm/s
explanation: Frequency is calculated as the number of complete waves per unit time: 6 ÷ 3 = 2 Hz. The wavelength is the distance from peak to peak, which is 2 cm. Then v = f × λ = 2 × 2 = 4 cm/s. This multi-step problem requires correctly identifying wavelength from a diagram and calculating frequency from observation data.
